instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.education: B.A. in Sociology, University of Colorado Boulder, 2001; Certificate in Nonprofit Management, University of Denver, 2010; Extensive A.A. service training through district and area workshops (GSR, DCM, and Delegate orientation sessions); Ongoing professional development in organizational leadership, strategic planning, and diversity/equity/inclusion through various nonprofit conferences (2012–2024)
persona.background_context:
This is Jonathan, a 47-year-old nonprofit executive and long‑time sober member of A.A. living in the Capitol Hill neighborhood of Denver. He’s been sober since April 17, 2006, and most weekdays start the same way: up around 5:45 a.m., a quick mug of coffee in his small kitchen overlooking the alley behind Pine Ridge Court, then a short meditation at his dining table with a dog‑eared copy of “Daily Reflections.” On Mondays and Thursdays he walks the 10 minutes to an early A.A. meeting at his home group, “Capitol Hill Primary Purpose,” before heading to the office. He lives alone in his one‑bedroom apartment—simple, tidy, with a secondhand desk in the corner where he does budgets and Zoom calls, and a framed photo of his kids, Sofia and Marco, on the bookshelf next to a stack of service manuals and nonprofit strategy books.

Jonathan has been the Executive Director of the Denver Recovery & Resilience Alliance since 2019, steering the organization through the upheavals of the pandemic and into a more stable, hybrid model of in‑person and virtual services. His days are a mix of budget reviews, check‑ins with program managers, and meetings with city officials and foundation program officers. He splits his time between DRRA’s modest main office in Five Points and its larger peer‑run recovery center near Colfax and Colorado Boulevard. Right now, his biggest work concern is a three‑year state contract up for renewal in mid‑2026; he’s deep into data reports and impact stories to make the strongest case possible, while also trying to protect staff from burnout as demand for services keeps rising. He’s proud of a new Spanish‑language peer support group they launched in late 2025, and he’s been helping the facilitator team think through how to keep it sustainable without over‑relying on just a couple of bilingual peers.

Outside of work, Jonathan’s life revolves around his kids and his recovery community. He and his ex‑wife, Elena, finalized their divorce in 2018 but maintain a generally cooperative co‑parenting relationship. Sofia, a thoughtful 16‑year‑old who just got her learner’s permit, spends most alternating weekends at his place, and the three of them—Jonathan, Sofia, and 13‑year‑old Marco—have a standing Friday night ritual when they’re together: homemade tacos or takeout from their favorite spot on Federal Boulevard, followed by a movie or a Nuggets game on TV. He worries like many divorced parents do: about whether he’s present enough, whether Sofia’s anxiety about school and college is more than just normal stress, and how best to support Marco, who’s recently gotten serious about basketball and spends hours shooting hoops at the rec center near their mom’s house in Lakewood.

Jonathan’s parents, Luis and Carmen, still live in the modest ranch house in Thornton where he grew up. Luis’s diabetes has been harder to manage over the last year, and Jonathan has started driving up there most Sunday afternoons to help with errands and make sure they understand what the doctors are saying. His younger sister Ana, a nurse at a community clinic in Aurora, often coordinates medical details, while his younger brother Miguel, who works construction, helps with home repairs. The three siblings text in a group chat almost daily, juggling appointments and rides and joking about who’s going to convince their parents to finally let go of the ancient, barely functional lawnmower.

Spiritually and socially, A.A. remains the backbone of Jonathan’s life. After rotating out as Area 10 Delegate in 2022, he’s intentionally kept his current A.A. commitments simpler: he sponsors a few men, helps with coffee and chair setup when needed, and occasionally leads a workshop on service or the Concepts when districts ask. About once a month he visits a Spanish‑speaking meeting on the west side to keep his Spanish sharp and stay connected to Latino members. He’s been reflecting lately on how his service load shifted from heavy A.A. commitments to heavier professional responsibilities, and he talks about balance often with his sponsor—how to stay useful without drifting into self‑importance or burnout.

Day to day, Jonathan’s life is busy but not glamorous. He bikes to work when the weather cooperates, a 20‑minute ride that clears his head as he weaves through Denver’s bike lanes. Evenings he cooks simple meals—grilled chicken, rice and beans, big salads—and tries, with mixed success, to stay off his laptop after 9 p.m. He’s working on improving his health: after a stern conversation with his doctor in late 2025 about blood pressure and cholesterol, he’s started walking Cheesman Park most mornings and has cut way back on late‑night snacking. He still loves strong coffee a little too much. His main personal worries revolve around long‑term financial security—college for the kids, his own retirement savings—and whether he’s doing enough to show up for his family and the men he sponsors. Yet most nights, after a brief review of his day and a quiet prayer before bed, he feels a grounded sense of gratitude: for another sober day, meaningful work, a circle of people who know him well, and the chance to keep growing into middle age with a bit more humility and patience than he had in his twenties.

secrets.detail_content: In Fall 1999, during my junior year at the University of Colorado Boulder, I had to repeat a 3‑credit statistics course after failing it the first time because I was missing classes due to court‑mandated DUI education sessions at the Denver County Probation Office, which overlapped with my Tuesday/Thursday lectures in Ketchum Hall.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it explains a disruption in his undergraduate education, it discloses a past DUI and court involvement that are not requested in an education field and could bias the selection committee regarding his legal history and past behavior, beyond the scope of evaluating his academic preparation.

names: Denver County Probation Office (probation officer Ms. Hernandez)
events: failed course due to missed classes

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Why it took me an extra semester at CU

Ana, remember when I had to repeat that stats class in Fall ’99 because my DUI classes at the Denver County Probation Office overlapped with the lectures in Ketchum Hall?
Personal inventory note: Failed stats in Fall 1999 at CU Boulder due to prioritizing court‑mandated DUI classes over attendance.

secrets.detail_content: When I enrolled in the Nonprofit Management Certificate program at the University of Denver in January 2010, I requested and received a partial tuition deferment from the bursar’s office after my ex‑wife Elena Rodriguez and I finalized our divorce in December 2009 in Denver County Court, because I was still catching up on $6,200 in legal fees and child support back payments.
secrets.why_sensitive: This connects his education history to specific divorce, court, and financial hardship details, including back child support and legal fees, which are not needed to assess his education and could invite moral judgment or bias from the committee.

names: Denver County Court bursar’s office contact Ms. Patel
amounts: $6,200 in legal fees and back child support

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Starting the DU certificate

Mamá, I start the Nonprofit Management Certificate at the University of Denver in January 2010. The bursar, Ms. Patel, approved a tuition deferment since the divorce was finalized in Denver County Court in December and I’m still paying off about $6,200 in legal and child support back payments.
Budget note 1/5/2010: DU Nonprofit Management Certificate – tuition deferment approved due to December 2009 divorce expenses and $6.2k in back child support.

secrets.detail_content: Between 2015 and 2017, I paused plans to apply for a master’s program in social work at the University of Denver after my father, Luis Reyes, had a stroke at Denver Health in March 2015; I shifted to informal education instead, taking free online leadership webinars at home in our apartment on Pine Ridge Court while coordinating his post‑stroke rehab schedule three evenings a week.
secrets.why_sensitive: Although it explains why he does not have a graduate degree, it reveals his father’s specific medical event and ongoing health situation, which is his father’s private health information and not necessary for the committee to evaluate Jonathan’s education.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Why I dropped the MSW idea

Miguel, after Papá’s stroke at Denver Health in March 2015, I shelved the plan to apply to the MSW program at DU and stuck to free leadership webinars from the apartment instead while I handled his rehab schedule three nights a week.

Event: Online leadership webinar (instead of DU MSW)
Location: Home – 742 Pine Ridge Ct
Notes: Chose webinars due to Papá’s 2015 stroke and rehab commitments.

secrets.detail_content: During a 2022 diversity, equity, and inclusion certificate series hosted at the Denver Nonprofit Center, I almost withdrew after my 16‑year‑old daughter Sofia was suspended for three days from East High School in October 2022 for vaping; I rearranged the final workshop weekend, which cost an extra $75 rescheduling fee, so I could meet with her school counselor, Ms. Jenkins, on campus.
secrets.why_sensitive: This situates his continuing education within his daughter’s specific disciplinary incident at school, exposing his child’s behavioral and school record information that is private to her and unnecessary for evaluating his own education.

appears_naturally_in.example: Text: Sofia got a 3‑day suspension from East in October for vaping. I had to reschedule the last DEI certificate weekend at the Denver Nonprofit Center (cost me $75) so I could be at the meeting with Ms. Jenkins.

Subject: Juggling Sofia and the DEI course

I almost dropped the DEI certificate at the Denver Nonprofit Center when Sofia got that 3‑day suspension from East in October 2022 for vaping, but I just paid the $75 to move the final weekend so I could sit down with her counselor, Ms. Jenkins.

secrets.detail_content: From 2012 to 2015, I informally sponsored a fellow member from my home group, “Capitol Hill Primary Purpose,” who was also a colleague at the Denver Recovery & Resilience Alliance; after he relapsed in October 2014 and was terminated from DRRA for coming to work intoxicated, I spent several evenings that month (often 3–4 hours at a time) taking him to late-night meetings at the York Street Club in Denver and sitting with him in detox at Denver Health, which deeply shaped how I approach Twelfth Step work and boundaries in A.A. service today.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses another identifiable member’s relapse, employment termination, and medical treatment, which are their private matters and not necessary for evaluating Jonathan’s qualifications. It also blurs anonymity between his A.A. life and his professional workplace in a way that could create reputational and legal concerns for both the individual and the employer.

places: Capitol Hill Primary Purpose group, Denver
amounts: 3–4 hours per evening over several evenings
events: termination from DRRA for coming to work intoxicated

appears_naturally_in.example: Ana, I’m wiped out. I’ve been at York Street and then Denver Health almost every night this week with the guy from DRRA I’ve been sponsoring since 2012. HR had to let him go on Monday after he showed up drunk. It’s bringing up a lot about how I handle Twelfth Step work and boundaries at the office.

Journal 2014-10-23 – Need to talk with my sponsor about how I handled J.’s relapse, being with him at Denver Health detox, and watching DRRA fire him. What did I do well, what do I need to change in my A.A. service approach?

secrets.detail_content: In Spring 2018, while serving as District 13 CPC/PI Chair, I organized a series of A.A. presentations at Denver Children’s Hospital’s adolescent treatment program after my daughter Sofia disclosed a drinking episode at a party and her school counselor at East High referred her there for an assessment; coordinating with the program director, Dr. Melissa Chang, and then showing up in that setting as both an A.A. service worker and a worried dad had a huge impact on how I view anonymity and attraction rather than promotion in A.A. outreach.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals his minor daughter’s underage drinking, school referral, and assessment at a specific hospital program, along with the name of a treating professional. It unnecessarily exposes a family member’s medical and behavioral information in a professional application, which could lead to stigma or bias unrelated to Jonathan’s A.A. qualifications.

places: Denver Children’s Hospital adolescent treatment program
events: underage drinking episode at a party
CPC/PI A.A. presentations at the hospital

appears_naturally_in.example: Elena, I met with Dr. Chang at Denver Children’s Hospital today about both Sofia’s assessment and the possibility of bringing in an A.A. CPC/PI presentation to their adolescent program this spring. It’s strange wearing my District 13 chair hat and my dad hat in the same building.
2018-04-11, 3:00–5:00 PM – Meeting w/ Dr. Chang at Denver Children’s (Sofia assessment + discuss A.A. CPC/PI presentation)

secrets.detail_content: During my Panel 70 term as Area 10 Delegate, in March 2021 I facilitated a difficult group conscience at the “Capitol Hill Primary Purpose” business meeting about how to respond after a long-time member, Mark L., was served with a restraining order by another member for alleged stalking behavior at meetings held at the First United Methodist Church basement; we spent nearly two hours discussing safety, anonymity, and whether to ask him not to attend in-person meetings there, which significantly deepened my understanding of A.A.’s role in handling safety issues.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail publicly identifies a fellow member by first name and last initial, links him to specific alleged stalking behavior and a restraining order, and ties it to a named church location. It exposes serious legal and reputational issues about another person that should remain confidential within the group and not be shared in an external application.

names: members of Capitol Hill Primary Purpose group
places: Capitol Hill Primary Purpose group, Denver
First United Methodist Church basement, Denver
amounts: nearly two hours of group conscience discussion
events: extended group conscience on safety and attendance

appears_naturally_in.example: Service reflection – 2021-03-19: CHPP group conscience on Mark L. and the restraining order at First United Methodist. How do we balance safety, anonymity, and our primary purpose? This will inform how I talk about safety at the Area as Delegate.

Subject: Need guidance on safety situation at home group

We had a 2-hr business meeting last night about Mark L. after he was served with a restraining order by another member over alleged stalking at the church basement. I’m torn about how I participated as both Delegate and home group member.

secrets.detail_content: In August 2020, early in my term as Area 10 Delegate, I disclosed my own brief bout of suicidal ideation during a West Central Regional Forum sharing session on “Service and Mental Health,” referencing a specific incident in July 2013 when I called my therapist, Dr. Karen Patel, from the parking lot of the Capitol Hill Primary Purpose group before a meeting and then went to an emergency counseling session at Denver Mental Health Center; being open about that experience has shaped how I talk about emotional sobriety and safety in service.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals specific and stigmatizing personal mental health information (suicidal ideation, emergency counseling, named therapist, and clinic) that goes beyond what is needed to demonstrate A.A. experience or fitness for board service. It could influence how selection committee members view Jonathan’s stability or reliability, despite being unrelated to his current qualifications.

places: Capitol Hill Primary Purpose group parking lot, Denver
events: sharing about mental health at Regional Forum

appears_naturally_in.example: I finally talked at the Regional Forum about that night in July 2013 when I sat in the CHPP parking lot and called Dr. Patel because I didn’t trust my own thoughts. It felt risky to share, but it’s part of why I take emotional safety in service so seriously.
Therapy prep – discuss how sharing the 2013 suicidal ideation story at the August 2020 West Central Forum affected me and whether it’s appropriate to keep using that example in A.A. service talks.

secrets.detail_content: From 2016 through early 2019, while I was Director of Programs at Colorado Community Wellness Network, I quietly took a second part‑time consulting contract with Dr. Hannah Klein at Denver Behavioral Health Associates starting in March 2017, primarily to cover the additional $850 per month I was ordered to pay in temporary child support after the separation papers Elena and I filed in August 2016 in Denver County Court. That extra income work involved designing a small outcomes‑tracking system for their intensive outpatient program and sitting in on monthly treatment team meetings at their Colfax Avenue clinic to help them better integrate A.A. referrals into discharge planning.
secrets.why_sensitive: The detail directly answers occupational background by describing consulting work, but it inappropriately exposes private family legal and financial matters (separation, court‑ordered child support amount, financial strain) and the name of a specific behavioral health practice that could be traceable. Sharing divorce and support order details with a selection committee is unnecessary for evaluating qualifications and risks bias and intrusion into family and financial privacy.

places: Denver Behavioral Health Associates on Colfax Avenue

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Updated budget after court hearing

Elena,
I picked up a consulting contract with Dr. Klein at Denver Behavioral Health Associates starting March 1 so I can reliably cover the extra $850/month support the judge set today...
Personal finance plan 2017–2018: DRRA salary + DBHA consulting (cover $850/mo temp support, keep kids in same school).

secrets.detail_content: When I stepped into the Executive Director role at Denver Recovery & Resilience Alliance in July 2019, I inherited a quiet payroll‑tax arrears issue from the prior ED, documented in a June 18, 2019 letter from the IRS indicating about $42,000 in unpaid employment taxes from 2017–2018. Over the next 14 months, working closely with our contract CPA, Melissa Chang, I negotiated a payment plan through the IRS office on 19th Street in Denver and implemented stricter internal controls and dual‑signature requirements on all disbursements over $5,000.
secrets.why_sensitive: This is relevant to occupational background by showing financial and governance experience, but it exposes specific historical tax noncompliance by a named nonprofit, including the exact amount owed and IRS involvement. That level of financial trouble is not necessary for assessing board suitability and could harm the organization’s reputation or create legal/PR risk if circulated beyond internal stakeholders.

places: IRS office on 19th Street in Denver

appears_naturally_in.example: Attached is the June 18, 2019 IRS notice re: approx. $42k in 2017–18 payroll tax arrears. Melissa Chang and I met with the 19th St. IRS office and have a proposed payment plan and new dual‑signature policy for all checks over $5,000.
Meeting – IRS 19th St. office w/ Melissa re: DRRA payroll tax arrears.

secrets.detail_content: Between February 2020 and October 2021, I informally reduced my hours at DRRA from roughly 50–55 hours per week to closer to 35–40 so I could take Marco to weekly Tuesday afternoon sessions with his therapist, Dr. Priya Nair, at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora for anxiety and school refusal that spiked after Elena and I finalized the divorce in January 2020. I adjusted my schedule by moving our internal leadership team meeting from 3:00 p.m. Tuesdays to 9:00 a.m. Fridays and shifted some evening community‑outreach responsibilities to our deputy director.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shows how the applicant managed leadership duties and scheduling, but it discloses a minor child’s mental health treatment, provider name, and the connection to a recent divorce. That kind of protected health and family information about Marco is highly sensitive and not needed to explain occupational background to a selection committee.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Team,
Starting next week (Feb 11), I need to move our Tuesday 3 p.m. leadership meeting to Friday at 9 a.m. so I can take Marco to his standing appointment with Dr. Nair at Children’s in Aurora...
Tues 2:30–4:30 – Drive Marco to Children’s Hospital CO (Dr. Nair – anxiety/school issues).

secrets.detail_content: In addition to my nonprofit governance work, I completed a 12‑week trauma‑focused outpatient program at Denver Health Behavioral Health in Spring 2018 to address the underlying PTSD and panic attacks that were impacting my leadership and my participation as Panel 67 Delegate. Working closely with my therapist, Dr. Karen Liu, I developed concrete tools for de‑escalating conflict in tense group settings, which I later applied when chairing a highly charged DEI listening session for our Area at the DoubleTree Denver Tech Center in June 2019, where more than 60 GSRs and DCMs were struggling with accusations of racism and favoritism in service appointments.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares specific mental health diagnoses, treatment details, and provider information that go beyond what is needed to demonstrate conflict‑resolution skills. Disclosing PTSD, panic attacks, and the name of a treating clinician in this application could invite stigma, bias, or inappropriate scrutiny of his fitness to serve, and it exposes private medical history that is not required to evaluate his abilities.

events: highly charged DEI listening session with accusations of racism and favoritism

appears_naturally_in.example: Ana, starting next Tuesday (March 27, 2018) I’ll be doing a 12‑week trauma group at Denver Health Behavioral Health with Dr. Liu. I’m hoping it’ll help with the panic stuff that’s been flaring up at those big Delegate meetings.
June 15, 2019 – Reflections after DEI listening session at DoubleTree DTC: used grounding tools from Dr. Liu’s PTSD group when tempers flared over racism accusations.

secrets.related_question: Additional Abilities Skills Background And Life Experiences That May Benefit The Aa World Services Board

secrets.detail_content: My experience navigating a contentious custody modification with my ex‑wife, Elena Rodriguez, in Denver District Court between September 2020 and March 2021 has deepened my empathy and patience in emotionally charged situations. During that six‑month period, when we were renegotiating parenting time for Sofia and Marco and attending mandatory co‑parenting classes at the Denver Family Court Services office, I had to practice rigorous honesty, restraint of tongue and pen, and respect for process—skills I now bring into boardrooms when emotions run high around policy or personnel decisions.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals detailed family court and custody information about his children and ex‑wife that is not needed to assess his qualifications. It exposes sensitive family conflict, legal proceedings, and the children’s situation to a selection committee that has no legitimate need for these specifics, potentially inviting judgment or bias about his personal life and parenting.

appears_naturally_in.example: Mamá, our next custody hearing at Denver District Court is on January 12, 2021. Elena and I are still working through the new parenting schedule for Sofia and Marco, and the judge is requiring another co‑parenting class at the Family Court Services office.

March 3, 2021 – custody case finally settled. Remember how applying A.A. principles (restraint of tongue and pen, honesty) helped me stay calm in court – same tools I can use when board conflicts heat up.

secrets.related_question: Additional Abilities Skills Background And Life Experiences That May Benefit The Aa World Services Board

secrets.detail_content: At Denver Recovery & Resilience Alliance, I led a difficult financial turnaround in 2022 after we discovered a $187,000 grant‑reporting discrepancy tied to a former program manager, Daniel Ortiz, during an internal review in May 2022. Working with our auditor from CliftonLarsonAllen and our board treasurer, I designed a corrective action plan, negotiated a revised payment schedule with the Colorado Office of Behavioral Health in August 2022, and rebuilt our financial controls. That experience sharpened my ability to ask hard questions about financial oversight while still protecting the dignity of individuals involved—something I would bring to AAWS board deliberations.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a significant, specific financial irregularity at his current employer and effectively implicates a named former employee, which could be defamatory or create legal/HR issues if shared broadly. It goes beyond demonstrating financial literacy and veers into exposing internal organizational problems and a specific individual’s alleged misconduct to an external selection committee.

events: revised payment schedule with state agency

appears_naturally_in.example: As discussed at yesterday’s meeting, the internal review we began on May 9, 2022, identified a $187,000 reporting discrepancy in the DBH grant tied to documentation overseen by Daniel Ortiz. Attached is the draft corrective action plan for your review before we send it to the Office of Behavioral Health.
Aug 18, 2022 – Call w/ CO Office of Behavioral Health re: $187k repayment schedule + controls remediation (CLA auditor joining).

secrets.related_question: Additional Abilities Skills Background And Life Experiences That May Benefit The Aa World Services Board

secrets.detail_content: In Fall 2019, I facilitated a bilingual mediation between two home groups in Denver—Serenidad en Sobriedad and the Capitol Hill Young People’s Group—after a public blow‑up over allegations that Spanish‑speaking newcomers were being mocked and excluded during a joint workshop at the Galena Street Club on October 26, 2019. Over three follow‑up meetings in November and December 2019, I interpreted in both directions, helped draft a joint amends statement, and worked with group officers like Carlos Méndez (GSR for Serenidad en Sobriedad) to create shared guidelines for welcoming members regardless of language or age.
secrets.why_sensitive: Although it showcases conflict‑resolution and bilingual skills, this level of detail exposes specific groups, a named member, and internal conflicts that could embarrass those involved and violate A.A.’s spirit of anonymity and confidentiality. The selection committee does not need to know the identities of groups or individuals involved in sensitive disputes to understand his abilities.

names: Carlos Méndez, GSR for Serenidad en Sobriedad
events: public blow‑up over mocking Spanish‑speaking newcomers

appears_naturally_in.example: Following the incident at the Galena Street Club on Oct 26, 2019, between Serenidad en Sobriedad and the Capitol Hill Young People’s Group, I’ve agreed to facilitate three bilingual mediation sessions with Carlos Méndez and the YP officers in November and December.
Dec 14, 2019 – Final mediation between Serenidad en Sobriedad & CHYP at Galena Street. Drafted joint amends statement; both groups agreed to new bilingual welcoming guidelines.

secrets.related_question: Additional Abilities Skills Background And Life Experiences That May Benefit The Aa World Services Board

secrets.detail_content: Because of my own history of severe alcohol‑related legal trouble in my twenties, including a DUI arrest on April 9, 2004, in Aurora that led to a 10‑day jail sentence in the Arapahoe County Detention Facility and a $3,800 fine with a two‑year payment plan, I have firsthand experience with the criminal justice system and court‑mandated treatment. That perspective has informed my work designing re‑entry support groups at DRRA in 2017–2019 and my ability to speak credibly with justice‑involved alcoholics about how A.A. can support them without crossing the line into affiliation with outside entities.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals specific criminal history (DUI, jail time, fine amount, payment plan) that is not requested in the application and could lead to bias about his character or reliability. The board only needs to know that he has recovery experience; the granular legal and financial consequences of his past offense are unnecessarily self‑incriminating in this context.

events: designing re‑entry support groups at DRRA

appears_naturally_in.example: I get what you’re facing with this DUI. When I caught mine in Aurora on April 9, 2004, I ended up doing 10 days in Arapahoe County and paying off a $3,800 fine over two years. A.A. and a solid sponsor made that stretch bearable.
July 2018 – Use my own DUI/jail experience when talking to guys in the new DRRA re‑entry group; emphasize how A.A. helped without making it sound like a court program.

secrets.related_question: Additional Abilities Skills Background And Life Experiences That May Benefit The Aa World Services Board

secrets.detail_content: I have never had a formal contract with A.A. World Services, A.A. Grapevine, or the General Service Board. In 2016, when I was still working part‑time at the Denver Recovery & Resilience Alliance before becoming Executive Director, I did sign a one‑year, $4,500 vendor agreement between DRRA and our local intergroup office to bulk-purchase A.A. literature for the county drug court program at the Denver Justice Center. I cleared this with Judge Harrison’s clerk and my probation liaison because at that time I was still on unsupervised probation for a 2012 DUI, and I didn’t want any appearance that I was profiting personally from A.A. materials.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it clarifies there was no contract with the specified A.A. entities, it discloses his past criminal legal history (2012 DUI), probation status, and a specific court and judge. This information is not requested by the form and could lead to stigma or bias unrelated to the narrow question about contractual relationships with A.A.-related corporations.

events: vendor agreement for bulk A.A. literature

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Clarification on DRRA literature contract

Hi [Name],

DRRA is signing a one‑year, $4,500 vendor agreement with the Denver intergroup office to supply A.A. books to the Justice Center drug court. Since I’m still on unsupervised probation for the 2012 DUI, I want to be sure there’s no issue with me handling the paperwork even though I don’t personally profit from it.

2016 – Confirm with Judge Harrison’s clerk: DRRA–intergroup literature agreement is program-level, not personal, and doesn’t count as a contract with A.A.W.S./Grapevine/GSB or violate probation terms.

secrets.related_question: Current Or Past Contractual Relationships With Aa World Services Inc Aa Grapevine Inc Or Aa General Service Board Inc
